title: Module Project: Start Volunteer Hub History
summary: Continue the parallel volunteer-hub repository through five cumulative steps to produce a trustworthy first snapshot on main with a repository-local identity and a clean working tree.

sketchBlocks.title: Prepare a trustworthy snapshot for volunteer-hub
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
This project guides you through turning an ordinary folder named volunteer-hub into a small Git repository with one clear initial commit. You'll act as a volunteer coordinator who wants a reliable local history for the project files (README.md, index.html, styles.css, and notes/schedule.txt). The five progressive steps are: inspect the folder, initialize on main, set a repository-only identity, stage only the authored files, and commit the snapshot named "Start volunteer hub".

Why this order matters: inspection before initialization prevents surprises; setting a local identity keeps commits attributable without changing your global Git configuration; staging lets you control exactly which files belong in the first snapshot; and a single, focused commit makes future history easier to read and revert if necessary. Each sketch below pairs with a hands-on Try It exercise that opens the volunteer-hub workspace and checks one specific repository transition.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspect the ordinary folder before creating history
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Before you run git init, look around the folder to confirm what's already there and to get comfortable with the state of untracked files. The goal is to be intentional: know the file names and their locations so you stage only the intended project files later.

What to check: current directory, the volunteer-hub folder contents, and the notes folder contents. A couple of useful commands are pwd and ls -R. Observing files now makes it obvious when Git reports "untracked files" after you initialize the repository.

Interpretation tip: Untracked files are just files Git doesn't know about yet. Seeing the README.md and notes/schedule.txt now means later you'll stage the exact same files into your first snapshot.

sketchBlocks.title: Worked example: initializing a different ordinary folder
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: here is a short, concrete walkthrough that shows the same idea applied to a different folder so you don't copy exact commands in the Try It. Imagine a folder park-log with two files. We initialize on main and check status.

```bash
# Move into the example folder and initialize on main
cd park-log
git init -b main

# Check repository status (shows untracked files)
git status --short
```

Explanation: git init -b main creates a new repository and ensures the initial branch is named main right away. The subsequent git status --short lists untracked files with a leading ? character; these are the files you will later stage. This worked example uses park-log and a different commit message scenario so your volunteer-hub Try Its practice the same skills on different files and a different project name.

sketchBlocks.title: Configure a repository-local identity
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A repository-local identity is an identity recorded inside this repository only. Use git config --local so you won't change the global git config used for other projects. For this course, the required local identity is:

- user.name: ZoeSkoul Learner
- user.email: learner@zoeskoul.local

Why it matters: commits record the author name and email. Using a repository-local identity keeps your global settings untouched and helps make each commit traceable to the intended project workspace. After configuring, use git config --local --list or git config --local user.name to confirm the values.

sketchBlocks.title: Stage the project files and record the first commit
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Staging is how you assemble the exact snapshot to commit. Use git add to place the four authored files into the staging area, then git status --short or git diff --staged to inspect the snapshot. When it looks right, run git commit -m "Start volunteer hub" to create the first commit.

After the commit, verify the one-commit history with git log --oneline and confirm the working tree is clean with git status --short. A clean working tree means your tracked files match HEAD and there are no staged or unstaged changes.

quizDraft.title: Inspect the Ordinary Folder
quizDraft.prompt: Open the workspace and inspect the volunteer-hub folder. Confirm the folder exists and contains README.md, index.html, styles.css, and notes/schedule.txt before the repository is initialized. Type the shell commands you would use to show these files and press Enter for each command.
quizDraft.hint: List the directory recursively and show the contents of the notes directory.

help.concept: Before initializing Git, use shell commands like pwd and ls to confirm the folder and files exist so you know what will be reported as untracked files after initialization.
help.hint_1: Use pwd to confirm your current directory and ls -R or ls volunteer-hub to list the volunteer-hub contents.
help.hint_2: To see the schedule file, use a command that prints its contents such as cat volunteer-hub/notes/schedule.txt.

quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.

quizDraft.title: Initialize the Repository on main
quizDraft.prompt: Initialize the volunteer-hub folder as a Git repository whose initial branch is main. Move into volunteer-hub, initialize on main, and run a status command to show untracked files. Type each command and press Enter.
quizDraft.hint: Use git init with -b main and then git status --short to see untracked files.

help.concept: git init -b main creates a new repository with main as its initial branch. After initialization, untracked files appear in git status output until staged.
help.hint_1: Change into the volunteer-hub directory, run git init -b main, then run git status --short.
help.hint_2: After git init -b main, confirm current branch is main with git branch or rely on gitExpectations verifying branch main for this exercise.

quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.

quizDraft.title: Configure repository-local identity
quizDraft.prompt: Inside the initialized volunteer-hub repository, configure the repository-local user.name and user.email to the required values: user.name = "ZoeSkoul Learner" and user.email = "learner@zoeskoul.local". Use git config --local and then show the configured values.
quizDraft.hint: Use git config --local user.name and git config --local user.email to set and verify the values.

help.concept: git config --local writes configuration that applies only inside the current repository. This is useful when a project needs a specific author identity without changing your global Git settings.
help.hint_1: Run git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" and git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local", then verify with git config --local --list or by querying each value.
help.hint_2: Make sure you run these commands from inside the volunteer-hub folder (cd volunteer-hub) so the settings are repository-local rather than global.

quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.

quizDraft.title: Stage the First Snapshot
quizDraft.prompt: Stage these four files into the index so they form a single proposed snapshot: README.md, index.html, styles.css, and notes/schedule.txt. Change into volunteer-hub, add only those files, and run git status --short to show they are staged. Type the commands and press Enter for each.
quizDraft.hint: Use git add with the explicit file paths and then git status --short to show staged (A) entries.

help.concept: git add moves files from the working tree into the staging area. Inspect the staging area with git status --short or git diff --staged to confirm what will be committed.
help.hint_1: Run cd volunteer-hub and then git add README.md index.html styles.css notes/schedule.txt, followed by git status --short.
help.hint_2: Staged files appear with an A or other two-letter code in git status --short; the grader checks that only the specified files are in the staging area.

quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.

quizDraft.title: Commit and verify the initial snapshot
quizDraft.prompt: Commit the staged snapshot with the message "Start volunteer hub", then show the one-line log and confirm the working tree is clean. Type the commands needed and press Enter for each.
quizDraft.hint: Use git commit -m "Start volunteer hub", then git log --oneline and git status --short.

help.concept: A commit records the staged snapshot permanently in the repository history. After the commit, a clean working tree means there are no staged or unstaged changes.
help.hint_1: Run cd volunteer-hub, git commit -m "Start volunteer hub", then git log --oneline and git status --short to confirm the one-commit history and a clean tree.
help.hint_2: The grader checks there is exactly one commit with the message Start volunteer hub, the tracked files are in HEAD, and the working tree is clean.

quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
